problem_text: Determine whether integral_0^infinity e^(-2x) dx converges, and if so find its value.

content.label: Improper: infinite upper limit
content.applies_when: the upper limit is infinite, so the integral is defined as a limit of proper integrals.

content.meaning: integral_a^infinity f dx = lim_{t->infinity} integral_a^t f dx.

content.label: rewrite integral_0^infinity e^(-2x) dx = lim_{t->infinity} integral_0^t e^(-2x) dx
content.action: rewrite integral_0^infinity e^(-2x) dx = lim_{t->infinity} integral_0^t e^(-2x) dx
content.purpose: convert the improper integral into a limit of proper integrals

content.label: the antiderivative is -(1/2) e^(-2x), so integral_0^t = 1/2 - (1/2) e^(-2t)
content.action: the antiderivative is -(1/2) e^(-2x), so integral_0^t = 1/2 - (1/2) e^(-2t)
content.purpose: evaluate the proper integral up to the cutoff t

content.label: let t -> infinity: e^(-2t) -> 0, so the limit is 1/2
content.action: let t -> infinity: e^(-2t) -> 0, so the limit is 1/2
content.purpose: resolve the limit to find the value of the improper integral
